MateoCV's blog

Codeforces Round 856 (Div. 2) Editorial
By MateoCV, 3 years ago,

Thanks for participating in the round! I hope you enjoyed the problems!

1794A - Массив префиксов и суффиксов

Solution

1794A - Prefix and Suffix Array

Observe that there are exactly two strings of length n−1n−1 (one prefix and one suffix). We will call them xx and yy. Then, ss is a palindrome if and only if rev(x)=yrev(x)=y, where rev(x)rev(x) is the reversal of string xx.

So, to solve the problem it is enough to find the two strings of length n−1n−1 and check if one of them is equal to the reversal of the other. This solution also works for any length greater or equal to ⌊n2⌋⌊n2⌋.

Intended complexity: O(n2)O(n2) per test case. (reading the input)

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main(){
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0);
	int t; cin >> t;
	for(int test_number = 0; test_number < t; test_number++){
		int n; cin >> n;
		vector <string> long_subs;
		for(int i = 0; i < 2 * n - 2; i++){
			string s; 
			cin >> s;
			if((int)s.size() == n - 1){
				long_subs.push_back(s);
			}
		}
		reverse(long_subs[1].begin(), long_subs[1].end());
		if(long_subs[0] == long_subs[1]){
			cout<<"YES\n";
		}else{
			cout<<"NO\n";
		}
	}
	
	return 0;
}
Feedback
  • Amazing problem: 77
  • Good problem: 321
  • Bad problem: 72
  • Didn't solve: 32

1794B - Не делится

Solution

1794B - Not Dividing

First, we add one to all the numbers in the array equal to 11. This uses at most nn operations. Then, we iterate through the elements of the array from left to right, starting from the second element. At each step, let axax be the element we are iterating. If axax is divisible by ax−1ax−1, we add one to axax. Now this element is not divisible by ax−1ax−1, because otherwise both axax and ax+1ax+1 are divisible by ax−1ax−1, but that means 11 is also divisible by ax−1ax−1 which cannot happen since all the elements in the array are at least 22 (because of the first step we did). This part also uses at most nn operations, so we used at most 2n2n operations in total. The resulting array will satisfy the statement property.

Intended complexity: O(n)O(n) per test case.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main(){
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0);
	int t; cin >> t;
	for(int test_number = 0; test_number < t; test_number++){
		int n; cin >> n;
		vector <int> a(n);
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			cin >> a[i];
		}
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			if(a[i] == 1){
				a[i]++;
			}
		}
		for(int i = 1; i < n; i++){
			if(a[i] % a[i - 1] == 0){
				a[i]++;
			}
		}
		for(auto i : a){
			cout << i << " ";
		}
		cout << "\n";
	}
	return 0;
}
Additional comment

Actually, the maximum number of operations performed by this algorithm is 32n32n. Try to prove it!

Feedback
  • Amazing problem: 193
  • Good problem: 408
  • Bad problem: 72
  • Didn't solve: 31

1794C - Оценка подпоследовательностей

Solution

1794C - Scoring Subsequences

We will first see how to find the cost of a single non-decreasing sequence s1,s2,…,sℓs1,s2,…,sℓ.

If we choose a subsequence with kk elements, to achieve the maximum score it is optimal to choose the kk largest elements. As the sequence is in non-decreasing order, the kk largest elements will be the last kk elements of the sequence. Thus, all possible candidates to be the answer are the suffixes of the sequence.

Now let's divide the ii-th element from the right by ii. The sequence now turns into s1ℓ,s2ℓ−1,…,sℓ−12,sℓ1s1ℓ,s2ℓ−1,…,sℓ−12,sℓ1. Observe that the score of a suffix in the original sequence is equal to the product of the suffix of the same size in the new sequence.

The original sequence satisfies s1≤s2≤…≤sℓ−1≤sℓs1≤s2≤…≤sℓ−1≤sℓ. It also true that 1ℓ≤1ℓ−1≤…≤12≤111ℓ≤1ℓ−1≤…≤12≤11 then combining these two inequalities we have s1ℓ≤s2ℓ−1≤…≤sℓ−12≤sℓ1s1ℓ≤s2ℓ−1≤…≤sℓ−12≤sℓ1 so the new sequence is also in non-decreasing order.

In order to maximize the product of a suffix in the new sequence, we will choose all the elements in the new sequence which are greater or equal to 11 (these elements form a suffix because the new sequence is non-decreasing). If there are elements equal to 11 in the new sequence, we have to choose them in order to get the subsquence with maximum length (among all the ones with maximum score).

Therefore, the cost of a sequence s1,s2,…,sℓs1,s2,…,sℓ is the maximum length of a suffix of s1ℓ,s2ℓ−1,…,sℓ−12,sℓ1s1ℓ,s2ℓ−1,…,sℓ−12,sℓ1 such that each element is at least 11.

Now, we have to find the cost of every prefix of the given sequence [a1,a2,…,an][a1,a2,…,an]. For a fixed kk, the cost of [a1,a2,…,ak][a1,a2,…,ak] will be the maximum length of a suffix of a1k,a2k−1,…,ak−12,ak1a1k,a2k−1,…,ak−12,ak1 such that each element is at least 11. We can find this length using binary search. Observe that we cannot compute the transformed sequence for every prefix, as that will be too slow. Instead, we can compute in each step of the binary search what would the number in that position be in the transformed sequence. By doing these, we can find the score of each prefix in O(logn)O(logn) time.

Intended complexity: O(nlogn)O(nlogn) per test case.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main(){
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0);
	int t; cin >> t;
	for(int test_number = 0; test_number < t; test_number++){
		int n; cin >> n;
		vector <int> a(n);
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			cin >> a[i];
		}
		vector<int> res;
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			int l = 1, r = i + 1;
			while(l <= r){
				int m = (l + r) / 2;
				if(a[i - m + 1] >= m){
					l = m + 1;
				}else{
					r = m - 1;
				}
			}
			res.push_back(r);
		}
		for(auto i : res){
			cout << i << " ";
		}
		cout<<"\n";
	}
	return 0;
}
Feedback
  • Amazing problem: 291
  • Good problem: 337
  • Bad problem: 52
  • Didn't solve: 61

1794D - Подсчет факторизаций

Solution

1794D - Counting Factorizations

First, we will count how many times each different element in aa occurs and check which of these elements are prime numbers. This can be done by checking for each element if it has a divisor up to its square root or using the Sieve of Eratosthenes.

To construct a number mm such that f(m)={a1,a2,…,a2n}f(m)={a1,a2,…,a2n} we have to choose nn elements of aa to be the primes in its factorization and nn elements to be the exponents. The numbers we choose to be the primes in the factorization have to be prime numbers and distinct. If there are less than nn distinct primes in aa, then there is no number mm satisfying the property. So, from now on we will assume there are at least nn distinct prime numbers in aa.

Let b1,b2,…,bsb1,b2,…,bs be the number of occurrences of each non-prime number in aa and let c1,c2,…,ctc1,c2,…,ct be the number of occurrences of each prime number in aa. After we choose the primes for the factorization, let c′1,c′2,…,c′tc1′,c2′,…,ct′ be the remaining number of occurrences of each prime number. As we can choose each prime number at most once, then c′i=cici′=ci or c′i=ci−1ci′=ci−1. For each way of choosing the primes, the number of possible values for mm is n!b1!b2!…bs!c′1!c′2!…c′t!n!b1!b2!…bs!c1′!c2′!…ct′! because this is the number of ways to choose where to place the exponents. The answer to the problem is the sum of all these numbers over all ways of choosing the prime numbers.

Observe that when we sum terms of the form n!b1!b2!…bs!c′1!c′2!…c′t!n!b1!b2!…bs!c1′!c2′!…ct′!, the value n!b1!b2!…bs!n!b1!b2!…bs! is a common factor over all these terms. Thus, we just need to find the sum of the terms 1c′1!c′2!…c′t!1c1′!c2′!…ct′! and then multiply by the common factor.

To find this sum, we will use dynamic programming. Let g(x,y)g(x,y) be the sum considering only the primes from the xx-th of them and assuming that yy primes need to be chosen (or that n−yn−y primes are already chosen). Then g(x,y)=1cx!×g(x+1,y)+1(cx−1)!×g(x+1,y−1)g(x,y)=1cx!×g(x+1,y)+1(cx−1)!×g(x+1,y−1). The value of g(1,n)g(1,n) will give the desired sum.

Intended complexity: O(n2)O(n2) (plus the complexity of primality checking)

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
 
const ll MOD = 998244353;
 
//checks if n is prime
bool is_prime(ll n){
	if(n == 1){
		return false;
	}
	for(ll i = 2; i * i <= n; i++){
		if(n %i == 0){
			return false;
		}
	}
	return true;
}
 
//computes b ** e % MOD
ll fast_pow(ll b, ll e){
	ll res = 1;
	while(e > 0){
		if(e % 2 == 1){
			res = res * b % MOD;
		}
		b = b * b % MOD;
		e /= 2;
	}
	return res;
}
 
vector<pair<ll, ll>> primes;
 
const int MAXN = 5050;
 
ll dp[MAXN][MAXN];
 
ll fact[MAXN], fact_inv[MAXN];
 
ll f(ll x, ll y){
	ll &res = dp[x][y];
	if(res >= 0){
		return res;
	}
	if(x == (int)primes.size()){
		return res = (y == 0);
	}
	res = fact_inv[primes[x].second] * f(x + 1, y) % MOD;
	if(y > 0){
		res = (res + fact_inv[primes[x].second - 1] * f(x + 1, y - 1)) % MOD;	
	}
	return res;
}
 
int main(){
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0);
	//reading the input
	int n; cin >> n;
	vector<ll> a(2 * n);
	for(int i = 0; i < 2 * n; i++){
		cin >> a[i];
	}
	sort(a.begin(), a.end());
	//compressed version of a, pairs {value, #occurrences}
	vector<pair<ll, ll>> a_comp;
	for(int i = 0; i < 2 * n; i++){
		if(a_comp.size() == 0u || a_comp.back().first != a[i]){
			a_comp.push_back({a[i], 1});
		}else{
			a_comp.back().second++;
		}
	}
	//computing factorials and inverses
	fact[0] = 1;
	for(ll i = 1; i < MAXN; i++){
		fact[i] = fact[i-1] * i % MOD;
	}
	fact_inv[0] = 1;
	for(ll i = 0; i < MAXN; i++){
		fact_inv[i] = fast_pow(fact[i], MOD - 2);
	}
	//adding only primes for the dp
	for(auto i : a_comp){
		if(is_prime(i.first)){
			primes.push_back(i);
		}
	}
	memset(dp, -1, sizeof(dp));
	ll res = f(0, n);
	//we have to consider the contribution of non-primes too!
	for(auto i : a_comp){
		if(!is_prime(i.first)){
			res = res * fact_inv[i.second] % MOD;
		}
	}
	res = res * fact[n] % MOD;
	cout << res << "\n";
	return 0;
}
Additional comment

It is possible to solve the problem with greater constraints, like n≤105n≤105. Try to solve it with this new constraint!

  • Good problem: 60

  • Didn't solve: 84

1794E - Разметка дерева расстояниями

Solution

1794E - Labeling the Tree with Distances

First, count the number of occurrences of each element in the list aa. Let these numbers be c0,c1,…,cn−1c0,c1,…,cn−1. Then, compute the polynomial hash of the array cc, that is H=∑i=0n−1cibiH=∑i=0n−1cibi where bb is the base of the hash. Because the tree is unweighted, there are only nn possible values to write in the unlabeled vertex (all integers between 00 and n−1n−1). Including this extra number, the hash has nn possibilities: H+biH+bi for each i=0,1,…,n−1i=0,1,…,n−1.

Now, let's compute the same hash for each vertex of the tree. That is, for each vertex xx count how many vertices are at distances 0,1,…,n−10,1,…,n−1, and if these numbers are d0,d1,…,dn−1d0,d1,…,dn−1 compute the value hx=∑i=0n−1dibihx=∑i=0n−1dibi.

To compute these values efficiently, we will use rerooting dynamic programming: We will make two DFS. In the first one, compute the parent of each node and the hash of each node only considering the nodes in its subtree. For each vertex xx, if we call this hash dpxdpx then dpx=1+b∑childidpidpx=1+b∑childidpi. In the second one (which must be performed after the first one), for each vertex xx compute the hash of the parent of xx considering the nodes which are not in the subtree of vertex xx. If we call this hash dp2xdp2x then dp2x=dppax−bdpx+bdp2paxdp2x=dppax−bdpx+bdp2pax where paxpax is the parent of xx.

Now, observe that hx=dpx+bdp2xhx=dpx+bdp2x. With these hashes, we can compute the good vertices. A vertex xx will be good if and only if hx=H+bihx=H+bi for some i=0,1,…,n−1i=0,1,…,n−1. Doing a two pointers algorithm with the two sorted lists h1,h2,…,hnh1,h2,…,hn and H+1,H+b,H+b2,…,H+bn−1H+1,H+b,H+b2,…,H+bn−1 is enough to find for each hxhx if there is a number on the other list equal to it.

To make the solution pass, it is advisable to use hashing with multiple modules or just one big modulo.

Intended complexity: O(nlogn)O(nlogn).

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
 
const int MAXN = 200005;
mt19937_64 rng(chrono::system_clock::now().time_since_epoch().count());
 
//Hashing stuff
const ll MOD[3] = {999727999, 1070777777, 1000000007};
ll B[3];
 
vector<ll> shift(vector<ll> h, ll val = 0){
	for(int k = 0; k < 3; k++){
		h[k] = (h[k] * B[k] + val) % MOD[k];
	}
	return h;
}
 
vector<ll> add(vector<ll> a, vector<ll> b){
	vector<ll> res(3);
	for(int k = 0; k < 3; k++){
		res[k] = (a[k] + b[k]) % MOD[k];
	}
	return res;
}
 
vector<ll> sub(vector<ll> a, vector<ll> b){
	vector<ll> res(3);
	for(int k = 0; k < 3; k++){
		res[k] = (a[k] - b[k] + MOD[k]) % MOD[k];
	}
	return res;
}
 
//Tree stuff
vector<int> g[MAXN];
 
bool vis[MAXN];
int parent[MAXN];
vector<ll> dp[MAXN], dp2[MAXN];
 
void dfs(int x){
	vis[x] = true;
	for(auto i : g[x]){
		if(!vis[i]){
			parent[i] = x;
			dfs(i);
			dp[x] = add(dp[x], shift(dp[i]));
		}
	}
	dp[x] = add(dp[x], {1, 1, 1});
}
 
void dfs2(int x){
	if(x != 0){
		dp2[x] = sub(dp[parent[x]], shift(dp[x]));
		dp2[x] = add(dp2[x], shift(dp2[parent[x]]));
	}
	for(auto i : g[x]){
		if(i != parent[x]){
			dfs2(i);
		}
	}
}
 
int main(){
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0);
	for(int k = 0; k < 3; k++){
		B[k] = rng() % MOD[k];
	}
	//reading the input
	int n; cin >> n;
	vector<int> occurrences(n);
	for(int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++){
		int a; cin >> a;
		occurrences[a]++;
	}
	for(int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++){
		int u, v; cin >> u >> v;
		u--; v--;
		g[u].push_back(v);
		g[v].push_back(u);
	}
	//calculating possible list hashes
	vector<vector<ll>> list_hashes;
	vector<ll> h = {0, 0, 0};
	for(int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--){
		h = shift(h, occurrences[i]);
	}
	vector<ll> extra = {1, 1, 1};
	for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
		list_hashes.push_back(add(h, extra));
		extra = shift(extra);
	}
	//calculating possible tree hashes
	for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
		dp[i] = {0, 0, 0};
		dp2[i] = {0, 0, 0};
	}
	parent[0] = -1;
	dfs(0);
	dfs2(0);
	vector<pair<vector<ll>, int>> tree_hashes;
	for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
		if(i == 0){
			tree_hashes.push_back({dp[i], i});
		}else{
			tree_hashes.push_back({add(dp[i], shift(dp2[i])), i});
		}
	}
	//calculting the answer
	sort(list_hashes.begin(), list_hashes.end());
	sort(tree_hashes.begin(), tree_hashes.end());
	vector<int> res;
	int pos = 0;
	for(auto lh : list_hashes){
		while(pos < n && tree_hashes[pos].first < lh){
			pos++;
		}
		while(pos < n && tree_hashes[pos].first == lh){
			res.push_back(tree_hashes[pos].second);
			pos++;
		}
	}
	sort(res.begin(), res.end());
	cout << res.size() << "\n";
	for(auto i : res){
		cout << i + 1 << " ";
	}
	cout << "\n";
	return 0;
}

      You could actually just use greedy for C using a pq. Add the new element at each prefix and while the lowest element in the given series is less than the size of the array, you will remove it. 196022463. Very easy implementation.

  • »

    n_k101
    3 years ago, show (+1) #
    »

    n_k101
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    can someone explain in problem B why can't we iterate from right to left basically how addition form left to right is making sure correct answer and not right to left

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      bestial-42-centroids
      3 years ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      bestial-42-centroids
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 3 →  
      0

      Because the constraint is from left to right

      Let me explain, draw the elements of the array from left to right in increasing order of the indices. For each i, add a directed edge from the i-th element to the (i+1)-th element. As you can see, the value of the i-th element is constraining the value of the (i+1)-th element to be not divisible.

      If you wanted to fix the issues by starting from right, then it would change a[i+1] for it not to be divisible by a[i] but then when you change a[i] for it not to be divisible by a[i-1], the fact that the value of a[i] changed might make a[i+1] divisible by a[i].

      While if you do it from left to right, the changes you do on the (i+1)-th value will not affect the values from 0 to i

      → Reply

← Rev. 3 →  
+1

O(N) solution for div2 C. Basically if a[i] = j, then for all k from i to i+a[i]-1, this index i will be the "bottleneck" i.e. our max length score will be k-i+1. We can iterate on i and maintain 2 pointers to avoid overlaps.

196049334

→ Reply

          aryanc403
          3 years ago, show (+1) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          aryanc403
          3 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          +3

          We can tweak Errichto algorithm in the above comment to generate a uniformly random prime.

          Just choose a random no in range and check if it's prime. If it isn't, keep choosing another random no in the range.

          A range of length N has N/logNN/logN primes so that this algorithm will end after O(logN)O(logN) iterations, and this will be a uniformly generated prime no.

          → Reply
          • »
            »
            »
            »

            3 years ago, hide # ^ |
             
            +24

            I agree.

            Disclaimer though: after generating a random prime, the computations modulo that prime are very slow. Do it only in CF, where you can be hacked. Otherwise, using a constant modulo is faster.

      Shreya1040
      9 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      ll mid = low + high/2; It should be -> ll mid = (low + high) / 2; (ll mid = low + (high — low) / 2, same thing, but is better, as it avoids integer overflow) If low is 3 and high is 4, your code gives 5, but it should be 3.

PurpleCrayon
3 years ago, show (+1) #
»
PurpleCrayon
3 years ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+91

I solved E by adding heuristics until I got AC. I challenge anyone to hack my solution: 196038364.

Here is what my solution does:

For each node

  1. Find the value that must be added to the array (by calculating the sum of distances from each node — the sum of the values in the array). Make sure that distance is [0,n−1][0,n−1]. Add that missing element.
  2. Check if the number of nodes with distance 00, 11, and 22 are correct.
  3. Check if the maximum value in the array is equal to the maximum distance from the node to any other node (can be done by just checking the endpoints of the diameter of the tree).

If at the end, there are ≤200≤200 candidates, check all of them individually. Otherwise, check any one of them. If that node is good, every candidate is good, otherwise they are all bad.

→ Reply

+16

How to calculate the probability of a hash failing for hashes like problem E?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    jeroenodb
    3 years ago, show (+8) #
    »
    »
    jeroenodb
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 4 →  
    +13

    I did the same as the editorial with 55 hashes, with P=109+7P=109+7 and 55 randomized bases. With a polynomial hash like this the chance of a collision is basically the same as the chance that for an arbitrary polynomial of degree nn, if you evaluate at a uniformly random xx that it is equal to 0modP0modP. A polynomial modPmodP has at most nn roots (if PP is prime). So the chance of a false positive for one hash is n/Pn/P. In this problem we actually check equality for n2n2 pairs implicitly. So this means that the final probability of failure on one test with 55 hashes is ≤1−(1−(n/P)5)n2≈1.3×10−8≤1−(1−(n/P)5)n2≈1.3×10−8. But seeing that solutions with fewer hashes also passed I think in general the n/Pn/P is a big overestimate.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      tfg
      3 years ago, show (+7) #
      »
      »
      »
      tfg
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      +10

      Are you sure that a polynomial mod P of degree n has at most n roots? That doesn't seem trivial to me. Also about the N^2 thing, if the values of b^e mod P are different, you're left with only 1 candidate per vertex after hashing once. That whole N^2 thing seems different from what I have in mind but it might be just different solutions.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        jeroenodb
        3 years ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        jeroenodb
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +20

        I think it's true for polynomials in arbitrary fields. The proof relies on the fact that if you have a root of a polynomial, then you can write the polynomial f(x)=(x−root)×g(x)f(x)=(x−root)×g(x), where g(x)g(x) has degree 11 less. By induction you can easily see it that the maximum number of roots is nn.

        → Reply

          »

          3 years ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          Nice. I was afraid of things not working as usual because x^(P-1) == x^0.

          → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        jeroenodb
        3 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        jeroenodb
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        Assume the answer for a testcase should be 0. Then for your answer on the testcase to be wrong, it only has to happen once, that any of the hashes of the nn rooted trees coincides with any of the hashes of H+bk,for 0≤k<nH+bk,for 0≤k<n (using editorial notation). So these are two (multi)sets of nn items, for each of the n2n2 pairs, you are basically subtracting the corresponding polynomials, evaluating the polynomials at random points, and hoping that the result is not 00, because then you mistakenly output some number of good roots >0>0.

        → Reply

+11

D can be solved in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) time using convolution + D&C. My submission.

Edit: Time complexity is not O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n). It is O(nlog2n)O(nlog2⁡n).

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    MuhammadSawalhy
    3 years ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    MuhammadSawalhy
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |

    → Reply

    • »
      »
      »
      Dragonado
      3 years ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      Dragonado
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +3

      Convolution is multiplying two polynomials using FFT. D&C is Divide and Conquer. They are very standard topics, you can Google them.

      → Reply

    »
    jeroenodb
    3 years ago, show (+1) #

    jeroenodb

      → Reply
  • »
    »
    User2147383647
    3 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    User2147383647
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Can you please explain further why fft and D&C come into play here?

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Dragonado
      3 years ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      Dragonado
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Since c′i=ci−1 or ci⟹ci!c′i!=ci or 1ci′=ci−1 or ci⟹ci!ci′!=ci or 1

      We want to calculate sum of 1c′1!⋅c′2!…c′t!1c1′!⋅c2′!…ct′!

      Let us multiply and divide the summation by (c1!⋅c2!…ct!)(c1!⋅c2!…ct!)
      We get (c1!⋅c2!…ct!)(c1!⋅c2!…ct!)⋅∑1c′1!⋅c′2!…c′t!=1(c1!⋅c2!…ct!)⋅[∑(ci1⋅ci2…cin)](c1!⋅c2!…ct!)(c1!⋅c2!…ct!)⋅∑1c1′!⋅c2′!…ct′!=1(c1!⋅c2!…ct!)⋅[∑(ci1⋅ci2…cin)] where c′ij=cij−1cij′=cij−1

      So now we want to select nn elements from {c1,c2,…,ctc1,c2,…,ct}, calculate its product, and then sum it over all ways of choosing nn such elements.
      To do that consider the polynomial f=(1+c1x)⋅(1+c2x)…(1+ctx)f=(1+c1x)⋅(1+c2x)…(1+ctx). The coefficient of xnxn in this polynomial is exactly the value we are trying to calculate.

      Multiplying two polynomials of length kk can be done in O(klogk)O(klog⁡k) time using FFT. But here we have O(n)O(n) polynomials. So if we just keep multiplying from left-to-right it will take O(n2logn)O(n2log⁡n) time. One solution is to use a priority queue and keep multiplying the smallest two polynomials in the product.

      The other solution is to use divide and conquer. Recursively calculate the product of the first ⌈t2⌉⌈t2⌉ polynomials and the last ⌊t2⌋⌊t2⌋ polynomials. Both polynomials are of size at most nn. Now use FFT to calculate product of the left-half and right-half in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n). Overall time complexity can be proved to be O(nlog2n)O(nlog2⁡n)

      → Reply
»

      I did observations from my local test case experiments. Whenever ans++, following condotion must met.

      int len = 1;
		RREP(i, n) {
			if (i>1) {
				if (a[i - len] >= len + 1) len++;
			}
			cout << len << " ";
		}
      → Reply

vaaven
3 years ago, show #
»
vaaven
3 years ago, hide # |
 
+7

Proof of 32n32n operations in problem B.

Proof

Let us say that first phase is making ai+=1ai+=1 if aiai equals 1 and the second phase is making ai+=1ai+=1 if aiai is divisible by ai−1ai−1.

Consider kk is the number of positions which equal 22 after first phase. In first phase we will make ⩽k⩽k operations (because we need to change only 1). In the second phase we don't know anything about other n−kn−k elements. That is the reason why we will do at least n−kn−k operations in the second phase. But what about our elements which equal two?

Consider the segment of our array where each element equals two. I say that in the second phase we will do ⩽k2⩽k2 operations on this segment. Why? If we changed element ii (now ai=3ai=3) then element i+1i+1 isn't divisible by aiai (because 22 is not divisible by 33). This is why the total number of operations in the second phase will not more then n−k+k2n−k+k2.

Total number of operations in both phases: k+n−k+k2=n+k2⩽32nk+n−k+k2=n+k2⩽32n

→ Reply

drexdelta
3 years ago, hide # |
 
0

HOW TO SOLVE PROBLEM D with N = 10^5 ?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    xox0
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    xox0
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +20

    Following the same setup as the editorial, we need to find the sum of all the terms of the form 1c′1!c′2!c′3!...1c1′!c2′!c3′!..., where cici is the frequency of the ith prime and c′ici′ is the frequency after we've chosen the bases. Let's look at this problem another way, let's look at the polynomial P(x)=(1c1!+1(c1−1)!x)∗(1c2!+1(c2−1)!x)∗...P(x)=(1c1!+1(c1−1)!x)∗(1c2!+1(c2−1)!x)∗...
    or in other words P(x)=Πallprimes(1ci!+1(ci−1)!x)P(x)=Πallprimes(1ci!+1(ci−1)!x)
    The coefficient of xnxn in this polynomial represents choosing the xx term nn times and the constant term the rest of the times, which is exactly what we want to do, we want to choose the prime as a base nn times. So if we choose our coefficients properly, this essentially gives us our answer. To calculate P(x)P(x) you can use FFT (actually NTT) and Divide and Conquer to do it in O(nlog2(n))O(nlog2(n))

    Submission

    → Reply
»

  • »

    atharv_tiwari
    3 years ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    atharv_tiwari
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +15

    Just had to correct the size of the dp array.
    The max value of n is 2022, but the max number of input elements is (n * 2), i.e., 4044. And since the value of the array elements can be up to 10^6, it is possible that we have 4044 distinct primes in the input.
    Thus, changing the size of the array to [4045][4045] made the code pass: 196195253

    → Reply

»
A_G
3 years ago, show (+3) #
»
A_G
3 years ago, hide # |
 
+29

I'll provide my generator for generating hacks on E, if anyone else wants to do more (and I know for certain that there are a lot more hackable solutions out there). Look for solutions that use some fixed pairs of base and modulo and place them in the pairs variable in the code. There were a few special cases that required another idea to hack but I won't describe the details unless requested.

Generator
import random

def conv(a, b):
    """
    Compute product of a and b as polynomials
    """
    c = [0]*(len(a)+len(b)-1)
    for i in range(len(a)):
        for j in range(len(b)):
            c[i+j] += a[i]*b[j]
    return c

def eval(poly, x):
    return sum(poly[i]*x**i for i in range(len(poly)))

def get_poly(x, mod):
    """
    Given integer x, return a nonzero polynomial P such that P(x) is a
    multiple of mod for mod on the order of 10^9.

    This algorithm fails in some specific cases but should be fine for typical
    values used for hashing. Adjust l and mx as necessary.
    """
    l = 10
    mx = 7
    res_to_tuple = {}
    while 1:
        coeffs = [random.randint(0, mx) for _ in range(l)]
        res = eval(coeffs, x)%mod
        if res in res_to_tuple:
            if coeffs == res_to_tuple[res]:
                continue
            return [coeffs[i]-res_to_tuple[res][i] for i in range(l)]
        res_to_tuple[res] = coeffs


# List pairs (base, mod) used in the hash
pairs = [
        (200003, 1e9+7),
        (200003, 998244353),
        (200003, 1070777777),
        ]

# Convert to ints (so you can write 1e9+7 instead of int(1e9+7))
for i in range(len(pairs)):
    x, y = pairs[i]
    pairs[i] = (int(x), int(y))

p = [1, -1]
for x, mod in pairs:
    p = conv(p, get_poly(x, mod))
if p[0] < 0:
    for i in range(len(p)):
        p[i] = -p[i]

S = sum(abs(x) for x in p)
# Number of vertices in the construction is about S/2
if S > int(4e5):
    print("FAIL", p, S)
    exit(0)
elif S > int(2e4):
    # output is too large to be pasted directly, use a generator instead
    print(p)
    exit(0)

# Print the test case given p
a = [max(x+1, 1) for x in p]
for i in range(len(p)):
    p[i] = a[i]-p[i]

p = p[1:]
edges = []
n = 1
for x in p:
    for i in range(x):
        edges.append((n, n+i+1))
    n += x
print(n)
a[0] -= 1
l = []
for i in range(len(a)):
    l += [i]*a[i]
print(*l)
for u, v in edges:
    print(u, v)
→ Reply
  • »
    »
    malachi_toney_goat
    3 years ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    malachi_toney_goat
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Can you explain how this generator works?

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      A_G
      3 years ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      A_G
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 4 →  
      +28

      For simplicity, consider a version of the problem where instead of n−1n−1 distances being given, all nn distances are given. It's pretty similar when it comes to hacking. A solution takes the following form: pick some rr and pp, and mark a vertex vv as good if

      ∑u=1nrd(u,v)≡∑i=0n−1rai(modp)∑u=1nrd(u,v)≡∑i=0n−1rai(modp)

      where d(u,v)d(u,v) is the distance from uu to vv.

      We'll try to make vertex 11 a false positive. Let PP be the polynomial defined by

      P(x)=∑u=1nxd(1,u)−∑i=0n−1xai.P(x)=∑u=1nxd(1,u)−∑i=0n−1xai.

      Then we should make it so that $P$ is not identically zero, yet P(r)≡0(modp)P(r)≡0(modp). Additionally, P(1)=n−n=0P(1)=n−n=0.

      There are pretty much no further constraints on the polynomials PP we can use now, aside from the coefficients not being too large so that the number of vertices is at most 2⋅1052⋅105. For example, if we have the polynomial P(x)=−10+13x−3x2P(x)=−10+13x−3x2, then we can write it as a difference

      P(x)=(1+13x+x2)−(11+4x2)P(x)=(1+13x+x2)−(11+4x2)

      which means a graph with $15$ vertices where 1313 vertices are at distance 11 from 11, one vertex is at distance 22, and the wrong distances consist of 1111 zeroes and 44 twos. The graph is easy to construct from here. (For the actual version of the problem, just delete one copy of 00.)

      To construct the polynomial, note that we can first find a polynomial with P(r)=0(modp)P(r)=0(modp) and then multiply it by (x−1)(x−1) to have P(1)=0P(1)=0. With pp on the order of 109109, we can use a birthday paradox approach. Generate random polynomials with small coefficients and check the residues P(r)(modp)P(r)(modp) until you find a collision P1(r)≡P2(r)P1(r)≡P2(r) where P1≠P2P1≠P2. Then we can use P=P1−P2P=P1−P2 as our polynomial. Assuming the residues are uniformly random, this should take about p–√p tries which is fine.

      To handle multiple hashes (ri,pi)(ri,pi), find a polynomial for each pair and then multiply them together. This works because if Pi(ri)≡0(modpi)Pi(ri)≡0(modpi) then any multiple of PiPi also satisfies the same property. At some point the product polynomial's coefficients are too large but I was able to hack 5 hashes pretty easily.

      → Reply

I'm stuck on problem A. I tried using the following code to find two substrings of length n-1 and check if one of them is equal to its reverse, but it didn't pass the test. Can someone help me

int main() {
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        
        vector<string> v(2*n - 2);
        for (int i = 0; i < 2*n - 2; i++) {
            cin >> v[i];
        }

        bool ok = true;
        for (int i = 0; i < 2*n - 2; i++) {
            if (v[i].size() == n-1) {
                string rev = v[i];
                reverse(rev.begin(), rev.end());
                if (find(v.begin(), v.end(), rev) == v.end()) {
                    ok = false;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }

        if (ok) cout << "YES\n";
        else cout << "NO\n";

    }
    return 0;
}

my idea for problem C using queue

    int n;
       cin>>n;
        queue<int> q;
        for(int i=0;i<n;i++){
            int x;cin>>x;
            q.push(x);
            while(q.front() < q.size()) q.pop();
            cout<<q.size()<<" ";
        } cout<<endl;

0

C can be done in O(N) in an easy way To get an answer as t we need to have at least t elements which are greater then or equal to void solve() { int n; cin >> n; int t = 0, mx = 0; vector mp(n+1, 0); for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) { // to get ans as t shold have at least t elemetms which is >= t int d; cin >> d; mp[d]++; t++; int tmp = t; t -= mp[tmp-1]; mp[tmp-1] = 0; mx = max(mx, t); cout << mx << " "; } cout << endl; }

→ Reply

Hussain
10 months ago, show #

Hussain
10 months ago, hide # |
 
0

In the second question, its easy to prove that total operations are at most 3*(n/2). In the first step we made all 1s as 2s, which results in n operations. Now for every pair of adjacent numbers in our array we only need to increase the first one because then the second one will then not be divisible and our work will be done. So at most n/2 more operations. Hence n + n/2 = 3n/2 operations.

→ Reply
